title: Object Queries
concept: Object queries are learnable embeddings that query the decoder to locate and classify objects across the image using attention.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does DETR not require Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)?

options.text: It predicts exactly one box per object natively, avoiding duplicates
It only works on images containing one object
It operates only in the frequency domain

exercises.explain: Correct! NMS is skipped because DETR is trained to output a single clean box per object.

cards.title: Asking the Right Questions
cards.body: How does a Transformer query an image to find objects? It doesn't use grids or anchors. Instead, it uses a set of learnable vectors called 'object queries.' Let's see how these queries act as scouts.

cards.title: Object Queries
cards.body: Object Queries are a fixed set of learnable positional embeddings (typically 100) passed into the Transformer decoder. Each query is responsible for asking the feature map: 'Is there an object in my specialized region/scale, and what is its shape?'

cards.title: The Roster of Scouts
cards.body: Imagine a panel of 100 scouts. Scout 1 is trained to look for large objects in the center. Scout 2 looks for tall objects on the left. Scout 100 looks for tiny items in the corners. They all look at the image together and report back what they find.

cards.title: Query Attention
cards.body: A street photo contains a car in the center. Object Query 45 (specialized in center-horizontal shapes) pays attention to the center features and outputs: 'Car found at coordinates [x, y, w, h]'. Other queries find nothing and output 'empty'.

cards.title: Fixed Locations?
cards.body: You might think each query only looks at a fixed pixel coordinate. Fact: Object queries are highly dynamic; they can look anywhere in the image using attention, though they naturally learn to specialize in specific areas and sizes during training.

cards.title: Object Queries in Decoder
cards.body: How object queries interact with image features:

left.text:
Learnable vectors entering decoder
No pre-defined grid shapes
Queries the image globally

right.text:
Queries search CNN feature map
Outputs box coordinates + class
Outputs 'no object' if empty

exercises.prompt: What are object queries in DETR?

options.text: A fixed set of learnable vectors that query the decoder for object features
Text prompts typed by the user to search the image
Filters that crop the input image scale
Weights used in the loss function calculation

exercises.explain: Correct! Object queries are learnable positional embeddings that scan the feature map for objects.

exercises.prompt: True or False: DETR typically uses a fixed number of object queries (like 100), meaning it can detect at most that many objects in a single image.
exercises.explain: True. The number of queries sets the maximum limit of detected objects (usually 100, which is plenty for most scenes).

exercises.sentence: Object queries scan the image features inside the Transformer ___ using cross-attention.
exercises.explain: Object queries enter the decoder and query the features extracted by the encoder.

exercises.prompt: What does an object query output if it finds no object in its region of focus?

options.text: It stops the forward pass of the model

exercises.explain: Correct! Empty queries output a 'no object' background class label, which is ignored during final display.

exercises.prompt: You are detecting a flock of 150 sheep using a standard DETR model configured with 100 object queries. What will happen?

options.text: The model will detect at most 100 sheep and miss the remaining 50, because the number of queries sets a hard limit on detections.
The model will automatically scale up to 150 queries.
The model will crash with a memory error.
The sheep will be grouped into boxes of 5.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The number of object queries is a fixed hyperparameter that limits the maximum number of objects the model can find in one frame.
